questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following correctly represents the variation of electronegativity (EN) with atomic number $(Z)$ of group 13 elements?
questions.solution: For group 13 elements, electronegativity decreases from B to Al. The decrease in electronegativity from $\mathrm{B}$ to $\mathrm{Al}$ is due to increased size of Al. The remaining three elements are more electro negative than $\mathrm{Al}$ and their electroonegativity increases from Ga to TI. Thus, the correct order of electronegativity of group 13 elements are as follows.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Amongst the halogens F<sub>2</sub>, CI<sub>2</sub>, Br<sub>2</sub>, I<sub>2</sub> the correct arrangement on the basis of increasing melting point is
questions.solution: Halogens in solid state are molecular crystals, held by vanderwaal's forces & their melting point increases down the group due to increase in size.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Identify the correct decreasing order of acid strength for the following compounds:

(I) HClO, (II) HBrO, (III) HIO
questions.solution:
The acidic character of hypohalous acid depends on the electronegativity of halogen. As the electronegativity value of halogen increases the acidic strength of hypohalous acid increases.

Here, Electronegativity of atom X ( Cl, Br or I) attached to O decreases down the group, polarity of OH bond decreases, acid strength decreases. It means more the electronegativity of the halogen atom, more the tendency to pull electron and weaker the O−H bond and hence proton (H + ) can easily be removed.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the correct order with respect to metallic property of $\mathrm{Zr}, \mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Sr}$ ?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Zr}, \mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Sr}$ belong the $5^{\text {th }}$ period and their order of arrangement from left to right is $\mathrm{Sr}, \mathrm{Zr}, \mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Sn}$.
Metallic property goes on decreasing as we move from left to right.
$\therefore$ Metallic property : $\mathrm{Sn} < \mathrm{Cd} < \mathrm{Zr} < \mathrm{Sr}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The amount of energy released when 1012 atoms of Cl vapours are converted into Cl- ions according to the equation Cl g+e-⟶Cl- g is 58×10-10 J. Calculate ΔegH- of Cl atom in kJ mol-1 or eV atom-1.
questions.solution:
Given:

The amount of energy released when 1012 atoms of Cl vapours are converted into Cl- ions =58×10-10 J

The amount of energy released when 1 mol (≈6.0×1023 atoms) of Cl are converted into Cl- ions according to the above equation is ΔcgH- of Cl vapours.

∴ ΔegH- of Cl vapours =-58×10-10 J×6×10231012

=-348×10 J mol-1=-3.48 kJ mol-1

We know that

1 eV atom-1=96.49 kJ mol-1

Therefore, ΔegH- of Cl vapours in eV

=-3.4896.49=-0.036 eV atom-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The correct statement among the following is:
options: <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>  salts act as good oxidising agents
<math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>  salts act as good Reducing agents
<math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>  salts act as good oxidising agents
questions.solution:
Sn4+ > Sn2+ (order of stability)

∴Sn2+ act as good Reducing agent

Sn2+→ Sn4++2eθ

+2 +4

Also, Bi3+>Bi5+Tl+>Tl3+ order of stability (Inert pair effect)

∴Bi5++2e→ Bi3+

+5 +3

Tl3++2eΘ→ Tl+

+3 +1

while in a group atomic size increases There Bi5+ and Ti3+ act as good oxidising agents

questions.q: The electron gain enthalpy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Δ</mi><mi>eg</mi></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cl</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>349</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the ground state energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cl</mi><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math>the ground state energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Cl</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is
questions.solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mover><mo>→</mo><mrow><msub><mo>∆</mo><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></msub><mi>H</mi></mrow></mover><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi><msup><mi>l</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>e</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mspace linebreak="newline"/><mtext>As ground state energy is</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mi>J</mi><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>l</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mspace linebreak="newline"/><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>=</mo></mtd><mtd><mo>∆</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>g</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>s</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mo>∆</mo><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></msub><mi>H</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo>∆</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>g</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>s</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub></mtd><mtd><mo>=</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mo>∆</mo><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>g</mi></mrow></msub><mi>H</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo>∆</mo><msub><mi>E</mi><mrow><mi>g</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>s</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>e</mi></mrow></msub></mtd><mtd><mo>=</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>349</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which from following statements is true for group 16 elements?
options: All elements of this group form $\mathrm{EO}_2$ type oxides
Oxides of all elements of this group are gaseous at room temperature
Reducing properties of dioxides of this group element decreases form $\mathrm{SO}_2$ to $\mathrm{TeO}_2$
questions.solution:
Besides $\mathrm{EO}_2$ type, Sulphur, Selenium, and tellurium also form $\mathrm{EO}_3$ type oxides.
Group 16 Elements: Oxygen and Sulfur are non-metals, Selenium and Tellurium are metalloids and Polonium is a metal under typical conditions.
All the elements of this group need 2 electrons to attain inert gas configuration either by gaining or sharing of electrons. Oxygen has high E.N so it gains two electrons to become inert gas configuration so all metal oxides are ionic and contains $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ in which the oxidation state is -2 . Oxygen shows $-1,-1 / 2,+1$ and +2 oxidation state. The tendency to show -2 oxidation state decreases from sulphur to polonium.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The IE1 of Ist series of transition elements are as given:

Ionisation energy in KJ/Mol Element Atomic number
0.65 Sc 21
0.64 Ti 22
0.64 V 23
0.68 Cr 24
0.72 Mn 25
0.76 Fe 26
0.75 Co 27
0.73 Ni 28
0.74 Cu 29
0.9 Zn 30

In general transition elements reacts:

1s2,2s2,2p6,3s2,3p6,3dn,4s2→1s2,2s2,2p6,3p6,3dn

How do you explain the ionisation of transition elements?
options: As the electrons are removed from the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi></math> orbitals, the energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi></math> orbitals become lower than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>s</mi></math> orbitals, therefore, during ionisation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>s</mi></math>electrons are removed.

When the electrons are introduced to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi></math> orbitals, the energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi></math> electrons become lower than that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>s</mi></math> electrons.

When electrons are removed cation having electrons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>d</mi></math> orbitals have lesser energies than the cation having electron in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>s</mi></math> orbitals.
questions.solution: Greater the value of Zeff on 3d electron than 4s electron shows that 3d electrons are tightly bound to the nucleus than 4s electron. Due to this different force of attraction between electrons and nucleus cations having electrons in 3d orbitals have lesser energies than the cation having electron in 4s orbital.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Value of shielding constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ne</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn></math>. Find the effective nuclear charge on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math>, respectively.
questions.solution:
Ne, Na+, Fe- are all isoelectronic species..

Electronic configuration of each species is given as

Ne10: 1s2, 2s22p6Na+11: 1s2, 2s22p6F-9: 1s2, 2s22p6

All have shielding constant.

Also, Zeff =Z-σ 

 ∴ Zeff Na+ =11-4.15=6.85

and  ZeffF-=9-4.15=4.85
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The atomic numbers of elements $A, B, C$ and $D$ are $Z-1, Z, Z+1$ and $Z+2$, respectively. If ' $B$ ' is a noble gas, choose the correct answers from the following statements
(1) ' $A$ ' has higher electron affinity
(2) ' $C$ ' exists in +2 oxidation state
(3) ' $D$ ' is an alkaline earth metal
questions.solution:
Given,
Atomic number of element $B=Z$
( $\because$ noble gas $\quad \therefore$ belong to zero group)
Atomic number of element $A=Z-1$
(ie, halogens)
Atomic number of element $C=Z+1$
(ie, group IA)
Atomic number of element $D=Z+2$
(ie, group IIA)
$\because$ Element $B$ is a noble gas
$\therefore$ Element $A$ must be a halogen ie, have highest electron affinity.
and element $C$ must be an alkali metal and exist in +1 oxidation state.
and element $D$ must be an alkaline earth metal with +2 oxidation state.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Which of the following statements is (are) incorrect?

(i) Percentage ionic character in CsCl bond is ~55% . (Electronegativity values of Cs and Cl are 0.7 and 3.0 respectively)

(ii) From H2O to H3O+ , the geometry around O atom changes drastically.

(iii) The most stable oxidation state for element with atomic number 113 is expected to be +2.

(iv) The 2nd ionization energy of Ca is greater than 1st ionization energy of it but lower than 2nd ionization energy of K.
questions.solution:
(a) Ionic character

% ionic character=16( x A − x B )+3.5 ( x A − x B ) 2

where, x A − x B =Difference in electro-negativity of bonded atoms. 16×(3.0−0.7)+3.5× (3.0−0.7) 2 =55.32%

(b) Geometry for both the species is tetrahedral, as in both 'O' is s p 3 hybridised

c 113=Rn 5f146d107s27p1= Due to inert pair effect 7 s 2 electron do not take part in bond formation, so, most stable oxidation state is +1.

(d) Successive ionization energies are higher for every element. 2nd ionization of K happens from 3s23p6 configuration which is an inert gas configuration and for Ca it happens from 4s1. So, K has higher second ionisation energy than Ca.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Identify the incorrect statement among the following.
options: Shielding power of 4f electrons is quite weak
There is a decrease in the radii of the atoms or ions as one proceeds from La to Lu
Lanthanide contraction is the accumulation of successive shrinkages.
As a result of lanthanide contraction, the properties of 4d series of the transition elements have no similarities with the 5d series of elements.
questions.solution: The regular decrease in the radii of lanthanide ions from La<sup>3+</sup> to Lu<sup>3+</sup> is known as lanthanides contraction. It is due to the greater effect of the increased nuclear charge than that of screening effect (shielding effect). As a result of lanthanide contraction, the atomic radii of element of 4d and 5d comes closer, so the properties of 4d and 5d transition element shows the similarities.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct order of increasing first ionization enthalpy for Ca, Ba, S, Se and Ar ?
questions.solution:
Ba < Ca < Se < S < Ar

Ba,  Ca belong to gr IIA ( alkaline earth metals)

Se and S belong to gr Vl A (Chalcogens)
and as we go down the group ionaization enthalpy decreases with increasing atomic size. So,
         
           Ba < Ca      and
           Se < S

Ar has stable completely filled electronic configuration and, therefore,has higest ionization enthalpy.Hence,the correct order is

Ba < Ca < Se < S < Ar in kJmol −1 503 590 941 999 1520
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Within each pair of elements <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> , <math><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> , and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>Li</mtext><mtext> </mtext></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Na</mi></math> , respectively, the elements that release more energy upon an electron gain are
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math>
<math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>
questions.solution:
(i) Electron affinity of second period p-block element is less than third period p-block element due to small size of second period p-block element, due to small size electron density is more and upcoming electron face extra repulsion, so energy release is less. Hence, EA order: F<Cl.

(ii) Down the group electron affinity decreases due to size increases. EA order: S>Se Li>Na.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Statement (i) $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{MgO} < \mathrm{ZnO} < \mathrm{P}_4 \mathrm{O}_6$-Acidic property (ii) $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{Cl}>$ Br-Electron gain enthalpy (iii) $M^{2-}>M^{-}>M^{+}>M^{2+}$-ionic size (iv) The second ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{Cu}$ is more than second ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{K}$. Which of the following is the correct representation of True $(\mathrm{T})$ / False $(\mathrm{F})$ for the given statements?
questions.solution:
(i) Acidic property increases with the increase in oxidation number of the central atom (element) and for same oxidation number acidic nature increases along the period (according to long form periodic table). So, this statement is correct.
(ii) Electro gain enthalpy for $\mathrm{Cl}$ is more (-)ve than of $F$ (due to extremely small size of $F$-atom) and electron gain enthalpy for Br-atom is less (-) ve than of $\mathrm{Cl}$-atom (due to its larger size). Hence, the statement in false.
(iii) If any ion accept the electron, ionic size will increase and if the electron will donate then ionic size will reduces.
So, the ionic size order will be
$$
M^{2-}>M^{-}>M^{+}>M^{2+} \text {. }
$$
Thus, this statement is correct.
(iv) For 2nd I.E, the electron is removed from $M^{+}$ion.
$\because \mathrm{K}^{+}$has fully-filled noble gas configuration while $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$has full-filled $d^{10}$ configuration and $\mathrm{K}$ has no $d$-subshell. Thus, 2nd I.E for $\mathrm{K}^{+}$is more than of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$ion.
Hence, statement (iv) is false.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
How many compound given below are amphoteric oxides?
\(\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{P}_4 \mathrm{O}_6, \mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{As}_4 \mathrm{O}_6, \mathrm{Sb}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{Sb}_4 \mathrm{O}_6, \mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\)
questions.solution:
The oxides of the type \(\stackrel{+3}{E_2} \mathrm{O}_3\) of \(\mathrm{N}\) and \(\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\) \(\left(\mathrm{P}_4 \mathrm{O}_6\right.\), dimer of \(\left.\mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)\) are purely acidic, that of \(\mathrm{As}\) \(\left(\mathrm{As}_4 \mathrm{O}_6\right.\), dimer of \(\left.\mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)\) and \(\mathrm{Sb}\left(\mathrm{Sb}_4 \mathrm{O}_6\right.\), dimer of \(\left.\mathrm{Sb}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)\) amphoteric and that of \(\mathrm{Bi}\left(\mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{O}_3\right)\) is predominantly basic.
The oxides of the type \({ }_{E_2}^{+5} \mathrm{O}_5\) are more acidic, e.g. \(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{O}_5, \mathrm{Sb}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\)
So, in the given set of oxides of group- 15 elements, two oxides are amphoteric, \(\mathrm{As}_4 \mathrm{O}_6\) and \(\mathrm{Sb}_4 \mathrm{O}_6\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following orders is incorrect with respect to the property indicated ?
questions.solution:
(i) Bond angle of NH 3 = 107 o

PH 3  >  AsH 3 = 90 o

According to bond angle the %s character of lp in NH 3 =22% while in PH 3  &  AsH 3 having approximately 100%, so the donation of lp in PH 3  &  AsH 3 are difficult. Hence, the acidic character increases and the order of acidic character is NH 3 < PH 3 < AsH 3 is correct

(ii) On moving left to right in a period ionisation energy increases. Li<Be<B<C but the first ionisation potential of Be is higher than that of B because Be has fulfilled configuration.

(iii) Metallic character increases hence the basic character of oxide increases.

(iv) In a group from top to the bottom, the number of the shell increases hence radius increases.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the symbol of element if it's atomic number is 116?
questions.solution:
\begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|c|}
\hline Atomic number & Temp. Name & Temp. Symbol & Name of the element & Symbol \\
\hline 109 & Unnilennium & Une & Meitnerium & \mathrm{Mt} \\
\hline 110 & Ununnilium & Uun & Darmstadtium & \mathrm{Ds} \\
\hline 111 & Unununium & Uuu & Roentgenium & \mathrm{Rg} \\
\hline 112 & Ununbium & Uub & Copernicium & \mathrm{Cn} \\
\hline 113 & Ununtrium & Uut & Nihonium & \mathrm{Nh} \\
\hline 114 & Ununquadium & Uuq & Flerovium & \mathrm{Fl} \\
\hline 115 & Ununpentium & Uup & Moscovium & \mathrm{Mc} \\
\hline 116 & Ununhexium & Uuh & Livermorium & \mathrm{Lv} \\
\hline 117 & Ununseptium & Uus & Tennessine & \mathrm{Ts} \\
\hline 118 & Ununoctium & Uuo & Oganesson & \mathrm{Og} \\
\hline
\end{array}

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Aluminium is usually found in <math><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> oxidation state. In contrast, thallium exists in <math><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></math> oxidation states. This is due to:
questions.solution: Reluctance in tendency of s-electrons to participate in bonding on moving top to bottom in p-block elements is called inert pair effect. Due to inert pair effect <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is very unstable in aluminium.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion (A) Boron has a smaller first ionisation enthalpy than beryllium.
Reason (R) The penetration of a \(2 s\)-electron to the nucleus is more than the \(2 p\)-electron hence, \(2 p\)-electron is more shielded by the inner core of electrons than \(2 \mathrm{~s}\)-electrons.
options: Assertion and Reason both are correct statements but Reason is not correct explanation for Assertion.
Assertion is correct statement but Reason is wrong statement.
Assertion and Reason both are correct statements and Reason is correct explanation for Assertion.
Assertion and Reason both are wrong statements.
questions.solution:
Assertion (A) Electronic configuration of boron \((Z=5)\) is [He] \(2 s^2 2 p^1\). So, in first ionisation ( \(1 \mathrm{IE}_1\) or \(\Delta_i H_1\) ) removal will take place from unpaired \(p^1\)-electron. Whereas that of Be will be from paired \(2 s^2\)-electrons which requires more energy.
Electronic configuration of \(\mathrm{Be}(Z=4):[\mathrm{He}] 2 s^2\) So, the Assertion is a correct statement.
Reason (R) \(s\)-orbital is being symmetrical in shape (spherical), it shields nuclear force (nuclear charge) strongly. So, \(2 p^1\)-electron of \(B\) is experiences lesser nuclear attractive force for ionisation. As a result,
\(\mathrm{IE}_1 \text { or } \Delta_i H_1: \mathrm{B}  < \mathrm{Be}\)
So, the Reason is correct explanation for Assertion.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Which of the following are correct?
(i) First ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{He} < $ Second ionization enthalpy of Li.
(ii) Li has the highest second ionisation enthalpy.
(iii) All $d$-block elements are transition elements.
(iv) The only alphabet not found in the periodic table is the letter ' $\mathrm{J}$ '.
(v) Francium concentration is $\sim 10^{-18} \mathrm{ppm}$ on Earth.
questions.solution:
All transition elements are $d$-block elements but all $d$-block elements are not transition metals. A transition metal is one which forms one or more stable ions which have incomplete $d$-orbitals. On the basis of this definition, scandium and zinc do not count as transition metal although they are d-block elements.
Thus, statement (3) is incorrect.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The size of the iso-electronic species <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> , Ar and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> is affected by:
options: azimuthal quantum number of valence shell
electron –electron interaction in the outer orbitals
Principal quantum number of valence shell
questions.solution:
For isoelectronic species the size is compared by nuclear charge.
Size of isoelectronic species is compared on the basis of their nuclear charge.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Fluorine is more electronegative than nitrogen. The best explanation is that
options: the valence electrons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> are on the average, a little farther to the nucleus than in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>.

the charge on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>,</mo></math> while that on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn></math>.

the nitrogen has half filled valence shell electron configuration, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>ns</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>np</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> where as fluorine has partially filled electron configuration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>ns</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>np</mi><mn>5</mn></msup></math>.
the electronegativity decreases from top to bottom in each of the group as the effective nuclear charge remains constant.
questions.solution: Generally electronegativity decreases down the group due to increase in atomic size and increases across the period due to decrease in atomic size and increase in nuclear charge.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Among the following, the pair of elements having same electronegativity values are
I. $\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{P}$
II. Be and $\mathrm{Al}$
III. $\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}$
IV. C and P
questions.solution:
Electronegativity is the tendency of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons.
Electronegativity of $\mathrm{H} \approx \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{Be} \approx \mathrm{Al}$ and $\mathrm{N} \approx \mathrm{Cl}$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Column I Column II
A. He i. Highest electron gain enthalpy
B. Cl ii. Most electropositive element
C. Ca iii. Strongest reducing agent
D. Li iv. Highest ionisation energy


The correct match of the contents in column I with those in column II is
questions.solution:
He⇒ Inert gas 

Cl⇒ Electron gain enthalpy is highest

Ca⇒ Most electropositive metal 

Li⇒ Strong reducing agent 

He has the electronic configuration of 1s2. It is completely filled electronic configuration, due to this, it has the highest ionisation enthalpy.

Cl ⟶ Cl -  gives the highest ΔegH as Cl– after getting one electron, it will achieve the stable electronic configuration 18Ar. Due to this, large amount of energy is released during this process.

20Ca⟶-2e-Ca2+ 

Alkaline earth metal and present in the fourth period, the outermost electron is far away from the nucleus. As a result, less energy is required to remove the electron and easily donate the electron, hence, shows maximum metallic nature.

E red o  of Li is –3.07 V, a strong reducing agent.

It has very high hydration energy due to smaller in size. The hydration energy is directly proportional to the charge density of the ion.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: In which of the following arrangements, the sequence is not strictly according to the property written against it?
options: CO<sub>2</sub> < SiO<sub>2</sub> < SnO<sub>2</sub> < PbO<sub>2</sub> : increasing oxidising power
HF < HCl < HBr < HI : increasing acid strength
B < C < O < N : increasing first ionisation energy
NH<sub>3</sub> < PH<sub>3</sub> < AsH<sub>3</sub><sub> </sub>< SbH<sub>3</sub> : increasing basic strength
questions.solution:
Drago's Rule :-

In case of NH3, PH3, - - -  bond angle decrease ,NH3  ( ≈107 ° ) & PH 3  ( ≈90 ° ) So, %s character of lp in NH3 is less & %s charactor of lp top to bottom increase so basic charactor decrease.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The correct order of decreasing acidic nature of oxides
questions.solution:
A binary compound of oxygen with another element is called oxide

Oxides can be simple (e.g., MgO, Al2O3 ) or mixed (Pb3O4 ,Fe3O4 ). Simple oxides can be classified on the basis of their acidic, basic or amphoteric character. An oxide that combines with water to give an acid is termed acidic oxide (e.g., SO2 ,Cl2O7,CO2 , N2O5 ). For example, SO2 combines with water to give H2SO3 , an acid.

 The oxides which give a base with water are known as basic oxides (e.g., Na2O, CaO, BaO). For example, CaO combines with water to give Ca(OH)2, a base.

Some metallic oxides exhibit a dual behaviour. They show characteristics of both acidic as well as basic oxides. Such oxides are known as amphoteric oxides. They react with acids as well as alkalies. For example, Al2O3 reacts with acids as well as alkalies.

There are some oxides which are neither acidic nor basic. Such oxides are known as neutral oxides. Examples of neutral oxides are CO, NO and N2O.

  Group 1 Group 2 Group 13 Group 14 Group 15
Period 2 Li Be B C N

According to periodic trend, the electronegativity of the period 2 elements increases in the order :

Li < Be < B < C < N

Electronegativty of the  central atom of the oxide is direclty proportional acidic nature of the oxide.

Therefore, the acidic character of their oxides decreases in the order as follows :

N2O3 > CO2 > B2O3 > BeO > Li2O
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
